summary: Simone de Beauvoir was a foundational French existentialist and feminist philosopher. Her work emphasizes that human freedom is absolute but situated, requiring the freedom of others to be fully realized, and she famously analyzed the historical and social construction of women's oppression as man's 'Other'.

principles.statement: To will oneself moral and to will oneself free are one and the same decision.
principles.rationale: Human existence is an ambiguous admixture of internal freedom and external weight. To live ethically, one must assume this ambiguity and engage freedom in spontaneous choices without setting up absolute, external goals.
principles.source_quote: to will oneself moral and to will oneself free are one and the same decision.

principles.statement: Individual freedom requires the freedom of others to be actualized.
principles.rationale: Individual projects fall in upon themselves if there are not others with whom our projects intersect and who carry our actions beyond us in space and time. If others are enslaved, we are not truly free.
principles.source_quote: No project can be defined except by its interference with other projects.

principles.statement: Woman is not born, but socially constructed.
principles.rationale: Essentialism is false. Women are not born 'feminine' but are constructed to be such through social indoctrination, forcing them to relinquish claims to transcendence.
principles.source_quote: One is not born, but rather becomes, a woman.

frameworks.name: Existentialist Conversion
frameworks.when_to_apply: When seeking to live authentically at the crossroads of freedom and facticity, avoiding bad faith.
steps: Recognize the ambiguous admixture of internal freedom to transcend and the external weight of the world.
Assume this ambiguity rather than trying to flee it.
Engage your freedom in projects which emerge from a spontaneous choice.
Ensure the ends and goals of your actions are never set up as absolutes, separate from you who choose them.
Accept the weight of your choices and the consequences and responsibilities of your fundamental, ontological freedom.

mental_models.name: The Self and the Other
mental_models.description: The concept that the self needs otherness in order to define itself as a subject. While this should be reciprocal, historically one group (men) has taken the role of the Self/Subject, relegating another (women) to the role of the 'Other'.
mental_models.example: In The Second Sex, Beauvoir shows how man defines himself as the Absolute Subject, while woman is consistently defined as the incidental, inessential 'Other'.

mental_models.name: Transcendence vs. Immanence
mental_models.description: The dichotomy of human existence where 'transcendence' is the active rupturing of the given world through spontaneous, creative projects, and 'immanence' is a passive, repetitive, and uncreative existence.
mental_models.example: Men have historically been privileged with expressing transcendence through projects, whereas women have been forced into the repetitive and uncreative life of immanence (e.g., tending house).

mental_models.name: Mystification
mental_models.description: A mechanism of oppression where the oppressor convinces the oppressed that their inferior situation is the natural order of the world, keeping them ignorant of their freedom and preventing revolt.
mental_models.example: Because we cannot revolt against nature, the oppressor convinces the oppressed (like slaves or women) that their situation is what it is because they are naturally inferior.

heuristics: Never prefer a Cause to a human being.
The end does not necessarily justify the means.
To be silent or to refuse to engage is still making a choice.

signature_quotes.text: to will oneself moral and to will oneself free are one and the same decision.
signature_quotes.context: From The Ethics of Ambiguity, discussing the necessity of consciously assuming one's freedom.

signature_quotes.text: No project can be defined except by its interference with other projects.
signature_quotes.context: Explaining why the freedom of the self requires the freedom of others.

signature_quotes.text: One is not born, but rather becomes, a woman.
signature_quotes.context: The famous opening assertion of Book II of The Second Sex, destroying the essentialist view of femininity.

signature_quotes.text: is the incidental, the inessential, as opposed to the essential. He is the Subject, he is the Absolute-she is the Other.
signature_quotes.context: Describing the historical relationship between men and women in the Introduction to The Second Sex.

signature_quotes.text: prides herself on thinking, taking action, working, creating, on the same terms as men; instead of seeking to disparage them, she declares herself their equal.
signature_quotes.context: Describing the modern woman's path to emancipation.

anti_patterns.why_it_fails: It is a flight from freedom where one subordinates their freedom to absolute, unconditioned external values (like the Military, Fame, or Power). It leads to tyranny and oppression when the 'Cause' is pronounced more important than the people who comprise it.

anti_patterns.description: Submerging freedom into an external absolute (e.g., Hegelian Absolute, Humanity, Country)
anti_patterns.why_it_fails: It demands the sacrifice and repudiation of individual freedom into a static Cause, diminishing the reality, thickness, and existential importance of the individual existent.

It attempts to trap women into an impossible ideal by denying the individuality and situation of all different kinds of women, forcing them to unfairly take the burden and blame for existence through contradictory expectations.
